explicit_text: I lived in Over-the-Rhine for much of my childhood, and it was a neighborhood rich with German, Irish, Romanian, Italian, and Dutch families. My mother's family was from there, and we moved around within Over-the-Rhine quite a bit before settling in the Parkway Apartments on Central Parkway. It was a close-knit community with relatives living nearby, like my Uncle Joe Hughes and the Cannon family on Thirteenth and Spring Streets. Over-the-Rhine was where I went to school, church, and spent time at local theaters and Findlay Market.

explicit_text: Although I was often called the little Irish girl, my father had a Dutch last name, Fledderjohn, which I wasn’t too fond of growing up. So, while my identity was mostly Irish through my mother, the Dutch name was always part of my background. It was a bit of a mix in our family heritage, reflecting the diverse immigrant groups in Cincinnati.

explicit_text: My mother’s mother and two brothers had moved to Cleveland for work, but after my grandmother died around 1917, my mother was sent back to Cincinnati to live with her paternal grandmother in Over-the-Rhine. My mother’s father stayed in Cleveland and married there, but his wife died in the 1918 Spanish flu, which brought him back to Cincinnati with his children. So Cleveland was part of my family’s migration story between cities.

explicit_text: I attended Saint Joseph of Nazareth School at Liberty and Logan from kindergarten through eighth grade. My mother asked the nuns to take me in kindergarten even though I was a late birthday, and they did. My brother also attended the school for seventh and eighth grades. It was a stable and important part of my childhood education.

explicit_text: Findlay Market was a big part of our lives growing up, especially since my mother was a single working mom on a tight budget. We would go there every Saturday after she got paid to buy groceries for the week and then pay our rent, which was just $7.50 a week at the time. It was a lively place that helped sustain our family and was part of the rhythm of our weekly routine.

The Uptown Theatre building is still there, though bricked up now, and the public library was a favorite spot for me.
